speaker: interviewer
text: August 10-- oh, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead. Just [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is August 10th, 1995. We are interviewing Henry Coleman, born Kalma. The interviewer is Dina Cohen. The interview is taking place in South Fallsburg, New York, USA, and it will be in English. My name is Dina Cohen. I am-- today is August 10th, 1995. I'm interviewing Henry Coleman, born Kalma, in South Fallsburg, New York. The interview will be in English. Could you please tell me your name and spell it for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Henry Coleman, C-O-L-E-M-A-N, previously Kalma, K-A-L-M-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you known by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Oh, yes. You mean full names, or-- yeah. I was called Nenek, which is N-E-N-E-K As my first name. But otherwise, I was called Hanok.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on March 17th, 1924, in Rypin, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-Y-P-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And what kind of a town was Rypin?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a small town. The population of the town was about 12,000 to 13,000, with about 2,500 Jewish families, affluent people. We had a big synagogue, several synagogues. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Most-- most of the people in town were either merchants or tailors or shoemakers or whatever other trades.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had three brothers and two sisters. I-- my father had two other brothers in town with families. He also had a sister with a family. And he had another sister in a neighboring town, which is Sierpc, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, before he married my mother, was a farmer, or his father was a farmer, my grandfather. When he married my mother, my mother was in-- in business. They had a shoe store. After I don't know how many years, my father got to learn the business and he ran the-- the shoe store with my mother together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that shoe store like? What kind-- describe it a little more, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were producing ourselves shoes. And my father was also buying shoes in Warsaw. But my two older brothers were making the upper parts for the shoes. And then the bottom, all parts, he was giving out to shoemakers to complete.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your home like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a very happy family. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] [CRYING] Myself and my younger brother [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] attended Hebrew school. It was a private school. My father was very active in Zionist organizations. My mother was also very active in WIZO, which was a ladies Zionist organization, which was a preliminary of the current Hadassah organization. And we had a lot of always guests in the house. We also, on Friday nights, it was a custom, my father used to bring some poor people for dinner, also Saturday for lunch. But we used to sit at the Friday night table 'til almost 11 or 12 o'clock at night. And we used to sing all kinds of Hebrew songs, Jewish songs. We had a very happy family. And my mother was very, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very guest-friendly, you know, and she always had people in the house. We always had a full table at the dinner table on holidays, on any other occasion, on Hanukkah, on Purim. It was a very happy life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please tell me the names of your parents and your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was shot right-- right away in 1939. October, 1939, the Germans took out some prominent Jews. They kept him for a while in a movie house for about three or four weeks. And then they took him out in a wagon. It was on a Saturday. I don't remember the exact date, but it was in October, because it was during the Jewish holidays. And they were taking him out to a nearby forest, about maybe two kilometers or three kilometers outside the town. And we heard shots. I-- in fact, I was the only one who followed the wagon. But I couldn't follow it all the way through, so I followed only for a certain distance, because they-- if they would have-- they would have seen me then, then they-- I would have been killed. So I followed as far as I could. But I heard the shots. And then we knew that they were all shot, you know. There were also a few Polish police officers in that group.

speaker: interviewer
text: But going back to before the war, just your family, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what-- just what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Israel Kalma. My mother's name was Nycha, N-Y-C-H-A. My oldest brother was Moshe, called Monek. My middle brother was Zalman [? Davek. ?] My youngest brother was Chaim. My older sister was Bella, or [? Baicha. ?] And my youngest sister was [? Marisha. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You said you went to a private Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was a Hebrew school, [INAUDIBLE] school. It was not an Orthodox school. It was a Zionist school. All the subjects were taught in Hebrew. We only had twice a week Polish. We had once a week English. And naturally, we-- we learned also the Bible once or twice a week. And during that-- that-- those-- those studies, we had to wear caps. And we were visited by rabbis from time to time. And, you know, we had-- there was a-- the school was very active in-- in-- in Zionist organizations. We had also, on Purim, we-- we put up a big show for the community. And I was-- I always had a great part in it, because I-- I used to sing. I was in a choir in the synagogue for about four years, so I had a good voice, and I sang. So-- and I was-- I was a very good student. And I always got always one of the main parts in-- in whatever show we put up-- was put up in school. And life was very-- you know, it was a lot of Jewish organizations in town. And we all-- in our house, you know, we had like when it came to elections for different Zionist organizations, it was a big split-up, because my father and my brothers were all in different organizations, so each one pulled towards the others for votes, you know. In general, it was a very happy life. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] We enjoyed living, you know. And the school, like I said, was a private school. And we spoke mainly Hebrew. My older brother spoke Hebrew too. And the emphasis on learning was to speak the language so you could, you know, speak well the language, not to mix it in with other languages. We also, at home, my mother spoke fluently German, in a few German dialects, because the area was saturated with Polish Germans. And the conversation in our business was also German, so my mother spoke several German dialects. We also spoke at home Yiddish, which my father spoke, my mother spoke. My sisters spoke Polish, because they were attending a Polish school, the public school. But otherwise, I would say mainly in my-- in my house the Yiddish language was spoken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a lot of friends when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, naturally I had a lot of friends, and a few of them-- I have several friends which survived the Auschwitz concentration camp. And I have also a few friends who were in Russia, which attended with me school together. Two brothers live in Netanya, which I met several times, being in Israel. Then I have a friend in Germany, which is from my hometown. I also have a friend in Belgium, in Brussels, which he survived. He was in the Russian army. And I have other Jewish-- other friends, not my age, but older, older men, you know, which one-- one of the men who survived the war, in fact, worked in the crematorium, survived. And he lives in Florida, which I meet whenever I'm in Florida. He-- he was a jeweler before the war, or worked for a jeweler. He didn't have his own business. So the Germans put him up there in the crematorium to-- to sort out all the jewelry that was taken away from the people who went to the crematorium. And I have other people from my home town which I met in Israel, which they emigrated to Israel before the war yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing up, did you have non-Jewish friends as well as Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Basically not, I wouldn't say, no. I didn't have any non-Jewish friends, because it was like a knit family. The whole town, each knew each other, you know. And we were-- actually, being that I was in Hebrew school, I had only Jewish friends, you know, from my school. I had some neighbors, which-- which were also Jewish, but they didn't attend that school. And they were alive, you know. But some of them passed away already since.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever come into contact with non-Jews before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to speak of, no. I mean, we met people. We saw them, because they were a majority. They were in the majority in town. But I never had anything to do with them, except that maybe in-- in-- in-- in the store, in my-- in our-- my parents' store. Or we had a-- we also had a store near-- in our house. We had our own house, a small house, a one-family house. We had another man who rented the space in our-- in our house, and he had a textile store. So I used to be-- on Friday, used to help him out, you know, in the store to watch if nothing should be stolen. And also in our-- in our own business, when it was busy, I used to help out. So I got in contact with some of the people, but very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever experience any anti-Semitism before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Anti-Semitism, yes. We had a lot of problems, you know, starting in 1938-- starting from 1937. They were coming in from different towns, you know. And also, people took part from our own town. And they were agitating the people not to buy from Jewish stores. And they were painting signs in front of Jewish stores not to buy from Jewish stores. That was the Jewish stores. And I remember well, my brother was at the time-- my younger brother-- my older-- middle brother was in the army. And he came once on-- on leave. So he, with other friends of his, were going around and as soon as they painted, they were walking right behind them and washing off the-- the signs. And there were many cases where they fought back with those Polish guys, you know, because they really were not afraid of them. They were really strong guys, and they were not afraid to put up-- you know, fight with them. But we had-- we had I think in 1939-- to be exactly, I don't remember. But it was in-- in either September or-- August or September, 1939, they brought in a lot of anti-Semites from neighboring towns. And on Fridays, you know, they had market days where they had-- in-- in the market, they had like stands, you know, with all kinds of merchandise, textiles, and ready clothes. They made their own like boots, you know. And they came in and they started to throw everything off, you know, and to beat people, you know. And the police never showed. Then they organized themself. Once they started to beat them back, the police showed up. But that's what they call the pogrom. That was the first pogrom we had in our town. That was in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was Polish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish people, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about early September, 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we-- we heard a lot about Germany. In fact, in our school, we were told to boycott any products made in Germany, because we already heard what's happening in Germany about the Kristallnacht. We knew, we expected that Germany will eventually invade Poland. So my father-- and we heard so many different stories about they're taking the young men away. They're sending them away, and nobody knows where.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father decided-- my mother decided that myself and my oldest brother should run away to a big neighboring town which is Wloclawek, near the Wisla River-- it was the biggest river in Poland-- because they were under the impression that over there, they'd go as far as that river. And from there on, the Germans would be defeated. In fact, my brother-- my middle brother was in the army at the time. And he also was of the same impression that the Germans were going to be defeated by this river. So we ran away to that town. And we spent there about two weeks. And it was very hard to get food. We had to stay in line to get bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Germans were bombarding already then from-- from the air and from artillery the town. The Polish army put up a fight. They were fighting for about 10 days. But then the Germans were nearby already, and we decided to go back home.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my-- my oldest brother, myself, and my father's middle brother, my uncle, went back home. Once we came home, the Germans were there already, because from what we heard, the German local population was armed. And before even the German army came in, they already took over all the institutions. They were already in charge of the town. So we came into town, and it didn't take long. The Germans came in like a couple days later. And there were right away signs and-- and bulletins out in the streets that Jews are not allowed on the streets after certain hours. They're not allowed to work the sidewalks, only the streets. And everybody kept their shutters close. And everybody was afraid to be taken in, because we heard already that they were taking in people to the previous police station.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the Gestapo was based there. And we heard that they're taking people into the basement. And they're beating them and they're torturing them. They're shooting them. But then, they were taking all the-- all the-- the young people had to report to a certain big park in town. They used to do all kinds of exercise with us, nothing really any work, you know, but exercise, all kinds of exercise. Then a few days later, they burned down the synagogue. And we had to-- they-- they took us to work there to take the brick outs, to separate the bricks. And from then on, we decided, or my family decided that it's time to-- to escape the town. But before that-- you know, that was after my father was shot. We got-- acquired a horse and wagon. And together with another family who were neighbors of ours, we were on our way to Malawa, where we stayed 'til 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Back to Rypin. When-- when you returned from Wloclawek to Rypin, did your older brother go with you as well? You had one brother in the army and one brother traveling with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Yeah. He-- he came back with me. My uncle and my older brother and myself, we all came back to town.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So your family was together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Except for my brother who was in the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's what I was confused about. And that-- when the synagogue was burnt, that was in early September, '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in 1939, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We saw the fire, because we lived in the-- in the front of the-- a few streets in front of the synagogue. But the synagogue was a tall building, and you could see the fire. It was a lot of wood in there, a lot of mahogany. It was a very beautiful, beautiful synagogue. It was painted by a [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] famous painter, all the murals. It was beautiful, a beautiful synagogue, with a lot of crystal chandeliers. And there were red carpet, you know, in the gangway and up to the altar, you know, And it was-- the synagogue was burning almost all day and all night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you said you were taken to sort the bricks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not only myself, but they took a lot of young people, you know, to-- to take the bricks apart, because they probably used the bricks for something else, because I've never seen bricks like this. The bricks were not the normal-size bricks that we see nowadays. They were about this size. And they were I would say four or five times the weight of a regular brick.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Germans probably had some kind of use for that, you know. That's why they make us take apart and clean the bricks up and stack them all in rows, you know. So later on, they-- they transported them. They took them away. And how many young people were taken to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Were about-- about 100 or 120 young people working there, because it was a big-- a big building. And there were, you know, building right next to it, too. It was like a [NON-ENGLISH], you know, like smaller, you know, places. They burned the whole thing down.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were under guard when you were doing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, naturally. There were-- there were guards there. There were German civilians, not-- not army-- army men. There were German civilians watching us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was your father taken away? Was that after the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, yes. After the synagogue, my father was killed. Like I said before, he was kept in a movie house with other Jewish people for about two weeks. In fact, we were contemplating to take him out-- to like take him out of there at night. But somehow, we didn't get to it. I don't know why. But then after two weeks, they took him out on a Saturday. They took him out to the forest. And they were-- that's where they were shot. After I was liberated and we came back home, we went to that forest, and we had-- we had to dig out the graves. But unfortunately, nothing was left, just the-- the head. We found it. The only thing we found is one man, which wore-- during that execution wore a rain coat. And in his rain-- rain coat pocket was a document stating his name, so we knew that was his name. So he was buried in the Jewish cemetery, because otherwise we couldn't bury anybody else in the Jewish cemetery, because there were Polish people among them, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how soon after that did your family decide to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We decided to leave, because we-- actually, it was-- it did get very bad. And we were afraid they're going to take my brother and myself, they're going to shoot us too, because they did take a lot of Jewish people to that place where I told you, where the Jewish-- where the police station was. That was where the Gestapo was based.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we decided-- you know, we left everything. We hardly took anything with us. And we went on our way to-- with another neighboring family to Malawa. How did you travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: By horse and wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your whole family was with you, except, of course, your father and your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Except for my father and my brother who was in the army. My two sisters, my-- my two brothers, my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did it take you to get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took us a couple days to get there. It was only about 50, maybe 55 kilometers from us, maybe 60 kilometers. And we came there, and we got, together with the other family, an apartment. There was no ghetto yet, so we lived in one-- in one apartment, two families.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide to go to Malawa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was the nearest, biggest town, because all the other small-- were small towns. Malawa was the only bigger town in the area. And it was closer to Warsaw, because we were contemplating to go to either Warsaw or Lodz, where there's a big concentration of Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And once you got the apartment in Malawa, what did-- what did you do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- in the beginning, there was a Jewish-- they formed already a Jewish committee, which was in charge of the Jewish people. But in the beginning, we didn't do any work. My oldest brother was trying to provide for us, you know, by buying things and selling things. But it didn't take long, and they formed a ghetto in a-- they formed a ghetto in like several, maybe seven, eight streets, like neighboring streets, you know. We had to move out of there. And then, naturally, we moved in only to one room. It was two rooms, but the one was another family, and we were in-- in the second room. So very, very small spaces assigned to each family. We stayed there and we-- they already were taking people to work. The Germans put in requests, not requests, but orders that each family should send out, you know, one man, depend how many men were in the family. But we got a notice that my older brother should report to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So being that my older brother was provider, my mother said I should. I was already then 15-- over 15 years old, and I was strong. So my mother decided I should report to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I did. The next morning-- I reported, and the next morning, they told us to-- to meet at a certain place. And with trucks, they brought us to a place called Nosarzewo, which was, you know, farms all around. And they-- they put us up in barracks. We were laying on straw, sleeping on straw. And they put us to work on a highway. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The-- there was old-- highways were very old, built yet in-- before 1914 by the Russians when they occupied that area, or the Germans. And they gave each two Jews, two men a space to dig out [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by-- I don't remember exactly the space, but it was very hard, you know. And they-- they gave me a pick, which the pick was very heavy. I'd never used a pick. And in fact, you know, there was a Polish man who was in charge of us. And he was standing behind me. And I was going with my pick and I hit him in his foot. And he almost killed me because of that, because that pick was so heavy. And I was hitting that pick into that highway, and it was jumping up. It didn't do any dent. But finally, after a while, you know, I learned the trade or the-- or the job. Myself and another man from a neighboring town were actually the-- the number one. We always finished first, because we had a certain piece of ground to do every day.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after a while, the man-- the head man, which was a German man by the name of Bergman-- and the name of the company that we worked for was named Strabag. It was a German company which built, apparently, highways. And that German man by the name of Bergman liked me so much that he decided that the only thing I'm going to do is take care of his shoes and all his chores, you know, to buy food by the farmers for him so he can send it to Germany. So I didn't do much work. I did work, but not much.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he allowed me on Saturday to go back to the ghetto, because on the roadway, a man which I knew from-- we lived in that apartment, and he lived in the yard of that same building. He used to pass by with his truck Saturdays, or every day. But Saturday, I stopped him once and I asked him if he would take me back to the ghetto, to-- to the town. And he said yes. So I was buying-- getting together some bread, some other thing, eggs and butter for my family. So I provided them with food, because it was very hard to get food. There were-- the rations were so low, there was hardly anything at all. So for a while, you know, I did that. But then they--
